firstLine: Unleashing the power of Claude Code Sub-agents
excerpt:
I discovered a single question rule for Claude Code subagents: does the intermediate work matter?

No? Delegate. You need the final result, not the noise in between. Send a subagent to explore your codebase or fetch docs and get a clean summary back. Your main context stays uncluttered.

Yes? Keep it in the main thread. You need to respond and adapt as things unfold. When iterating on a failing test or chasing a bug, you need the full output, not a sanitized summary. Hiding the details kills your ability to react.

Code reviews are where this gets interesting. Assign a reviewer subagent after you have built a feature. Your main thread lived through every decision, so it is blind to weaknesses. A subagent operates in fresh context with no history. Load its system prompt with your custom rules (e.g. "Verify every new route has auth middleware and flag any secrets that could leak into logs") and it catches what your conversation would miss.

I currently use subagents for 3 things:

1. Exploratory work: Keeps the main thread clean and synthesized.
2. Custom context: For tasks needing strict instructions like domain rules and security checks.
3. Code reviews: Fresh eyes with zero build bias.

Watch for these 3 traps:

1. Expert personas do not add capability: There's nothing a so-called "expert" sub-agent can do that your main thread can't.
2. Multistep pipelines: When steps depend on each other, they hide the details you need to steer.
3. Running tests via subagents: This gives you sanitized output when you actually need the raw stack trace to debug.

The decision rule is simple: No intermediate work? Delegate. Otherwise, stay in the main conversation.

If you are not running custom review subagents yet, run /agents inside Claude Code. It walks you through creating one in under a minute (see the subagent in action in the video below).

What custom subagents are you building right now? Let me know in the comments.

#Anthropic #ClaudeCode #CodingAgents #AI #SubAgents

firstLine: Context bloat is quietly wrecking your coding agent.
excerpt:
Context bloat is quietly wrecking your coding agent. Context engineering is how you fix it.

I

Build agents with agents 🤖

Every developer building AI agents today hits the same 2 walls.

1. The tooling is fragmented. You need one tool to scaffold, another to evaluate, another to deploy, another to monitor. You spend more time gluing them together than building the actual agent.

2. When they ask their coding agent for help, it hallucinates. They spend half their day in a loop of "no, that

firstLine: The biggest blind spot in AI for incident response is simple: it does not know what your team already learned.
excerpt:
The biggest blind spot in AI for incident response is simple: it does not know what your team already learned. So on-call engineers get sent down paths their teammates already ruled out last month.

I built a multi-agent system that starts with your team

firstLine: I vibe coded a GCP Advisor agent that never guesses.
excerpt:
I just vibe coded a GCP Advisor agent using Gemini CLI and ADK agent skills, applying context engineering to go from prototype to production grade - powered by Google managed Developer Knowledge MCP server.

And it never guesses.

Vibe coding gets you a prototype fast. But it cuts corners - hallucinated APIs, invented patterns, scaffolding that looks right until it doesn't.

ADK agent skills fix that. They give Gemini CLI the right context to build correctly - architecture patterns, tool specs, evaluation criteria. That's what takes you from prototype to production grade.

firstLine: I built an agent that reads every GCP release note so you don't have to.
excerpt:
I built an agent that reads every GCP release note so you don't have to.

Meet ReleaseLens: an AI agent I built to monitor Google Cloud release notes, filter out the noise and tell developers exactly how updates impact their specific tech stacks.
